title: Abdominal swelling and right upper quadrant pain in a young woman

chart.cc: Abdominal swelling and right upper quadrant pain
chart.oneLiner: 32F on combined oral contraceptives presents with 3 weeks of progressive abdominal distension, right upper quadrant pain, and leg swelling.

opening: My belly has been swelling up and aching on the right side for a few weeks, and now my legs are puffy too.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 32-year-old woman, uncomfortable from a distended abdomen, anxious about the swelling. Cooperative and a good historian once asked.

HPI: 3 weeks of progressive abdominal distension with dull, constant right upper quadrant pain and early satiety. Legs have become swollen over the last week. Mild nausea; no vomiting. NO confusion at baseline (watch for encephalopathy). NO black/bloody stools or hematemesis (watch for variceal bleeding). She has had two early miscarriages in the past (only mentions if asked about pregnancy/obstetric history). She started a combined oral contraceptive pill about 8 months ago (only quantifies/mentions if asked about medications or contraception).
PMH: two first-trimester pregnancy losses; otherwise healthy; NO known liver disease, NO prior clots that she knows of. FAMILY: a sister with blood clots in the legs and a brother with a "thick blood" disorder (only if asked about family history of clots). MEDS: combined oral contraceptive pill for 8 months (if asked). SOCIAL: minimal alcohol (a few drinks per month), no IV drugs, no tattoos, no high-risk exposures.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: comfortable at rest but visibly distended abdomen; mild scleral icterus.
- Vitals: BP 108/70, HR 104, RR 18, 37.2°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Abdomen: tender, smooth hepatomegaly in the RUQ; tense ascites with shifting dullness and a fluid wave if examined; no splenomegaly appreciated.
- Extremities: bilateral lower-extremity pitting edema.
- Neuro: alert and oriented; NO asterixis if examined.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the oral contraceptive use, the prior miscarriages, or the family history of clots unless asked about medications/contraception, obstetric history, or family clotting history respectively.
- Ascites, hepatomegaly, and edema are revealed only if the relevant area is examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.LFTs: AST 180, ALT 165 (modest hepatocellular elevation); ALP and GGT mildly elevated; an acute ischemic-hepatitis pattern can also occur with rapid outflow obstruction.
labs.Bilirubin: Total bilirubin mildly-to-moderately elevated; albumin low-normal.
labs.INR: Mildly prolonged (early impaired synthetic function); platelets often normal or HIGH.
labs.AscitesFluid: Diagnostic paracentesis: SAAG HIGH (>=1.1 g/dL, portal hypertension) with a HIGH ascitic total protein (>2.5 g/dL) — pattern typical of post-sinusoidal/hepatic outflow obstruction.
labs.DopplerUS: Hepatic vein Doppler ultrasound: absent/reversed/turbulent flow in the hepatic veins with hepatic vein and/or IVC thrombosis, caudate lobe hypertrophy, and ascites — CONFIRMS hepatic venous outflow obstruction.
labs.Thrombophilia: JAK2 V617F mutation POSITIVE (occult myeloproliferative neoplasm); antiphospholipid antibodies sent — establishes the underlying prothrombotic cause.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia at ~104 bpm with low QRS voltage in the limb leads from the large-volume ascites; no acute ischemic changes.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Sinus tachycardia with low limb-lead QRS voltage; no acute ischemic changes. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

searchTerms: low voltage QRS limb leads ECG

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Elevated hemidiaphragms with low lung volumes from abdominal distension and a small right pleural effusion (hepatic hydrothorax); no consolidation.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Elevated hemidiaphragms with low lung volumes and a small right pleural effusion. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph with elevated hemidiaphragms and small right pleural effusion
searchTerms: small right pleural effusion chest x-ray
elevated hemidiaphragm low lung volumes radiograph

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this new-onset ascites and hepatomegaly (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Budd-Chiari syndrome (hepatic venous outflow obstruction) (leading,7). Acute/fulminant viral or toxic hepatitis (CANT-MISS to exclude,4). Cirrhosis with decompensation / portal vein thrombosis (CANT-MISS,3). Right-heart failure / constrictive pericarditis (CANT-MISS,3). Malignancy with hepatic vein invasion (e.g., HCC) or peritoneal disease (core,3). Names hepatic venous outflow obstruction with the triad of abdominal pain, ascites, and tender hepatomegaly in a prothrombotic young woman (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Budd-Chiari syndrome (hepatic venous outflow obstruction). Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Acute/fulminant viral or toxic hepatitis; Cirrhosis with decompensation / portal vein thrombosis; Right-heart failure / constrictive pericarditis. Also reasonable: Malignancy with hepatic vein invasion (e.g., HCC) or peritoneal carcinomatosis.

criticalActions.item: Cirrhosis with decompensation / portal vein thrombosis

coreActions.item: Malignancy with hepatic vein invasion (HCC) or peritoneal carcinomatosis
Names hepatic venous outflow obstruction with the triad of pain, ascites, and tender hepatomegaly in a prothrombotic young woman

mustNotMiss: Cirrhosis with decompensation / portal vein thrombosis
acceptableAlternatives: Malignancy with hepatic vein invasion (HCC) or peritoneal carcinomatosis

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Hepatic vein/IVC Doppler ultrasound — first-line imaging for outflow obstruction (CANT-MISS,4). Diagnostic paracentesis with SAAG and ascitic protein (CANT-MISS,3). LFTs, bilirubin, INR/albumin — pattern and synthetic function (2). CBC and thrombophilia workup incl. JAK2 V617F and antiphospholipid antibodies (CANT-MISS,3). Viral hepatitis serologies / acetaminophen level to exclude other hepatocellular injury (2). Echocardiogram if right-heart failure suspected (1). PENALTY: anchoring on simple cirrhosis and not imaging the hepatic veins (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; LFTs; Bilirubin; INR; Ascites fluid; Doppler US; Thrombophilia.

criticalActions.item: Diagnostic paracentesis with SAAG and ascitic protein

penalties.item: anchoring on simple cirrhosis and not imaging the hepatic veins

requiredTests: Diagnostic paracentesis with SAAG and ascitic protein
contraindicatedTests: anchoring on simple cirrhosis and not imaging the hepatic veins

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia with low limb-lead voltage from large-volume ascites; no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (sinus tachycardia) (7); recognizes low voltage and attributes it to body habitus/effusion/ascites rather than cardiac pathology, and notes no acute ischemia (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia or pericardial disease without correlation (-3).
keyFindings: low QRS voltage in the limb leads attributable to ascites/effusion
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia with low limb-lead QRS voltage; no acute ischemic changes.
commonMistakes: calling low voltage a primary cardiac/pericardial process without clinical correlation
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage in the limb leads (large-volume ascites/effusion); no acute ischemic changes.

criticalActions: Sinus tachycardia with low limb-lead QRS voltage; no acute ischemic changes.
coreActions: low QRS voltage in the limb leads attributable to ascites/effusion

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = elevated hemidiaphragms with low lung volumes and a small right pleural effusion (hepatic hydrothorax); no consolidation. CREDIT: identifies the small effusion / elevated hemidiaphragms (6); links it to the abdominal distension / hepatic hydrothorax (4). PENALTY: inventing consolidation/pneumonia (-2).
keyFindings: elevated hemidiaphragms with low lung volumes from ascites
criticalFindings: Small right pleural effusion with elevated hemidiaphragms (hepatic hydrothorax).
commonMistakes: mistaking the small effusion or raised diaphragm for basal consolidation
steps.idealAnswer: Elevated hemidiaphragms with low lung volumes and a small right pleural effusion (hepatic hydrothorax); lungs otherwise clear.

criticalActions: Small right pleural effusion with elevated hemidiaphragms (hepatic hydrothorax).
coreActions: elevated hemidiaphragms with low lung volumes from ascites

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Doppler shows absent/reversed flow in the hepatic veins with caudate lobe hypertrophy, ascites is a high-SAAG high-protein fluid, and JAK2 V617F is positive. What is your diagnosis and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Budd-Chiari syndrome (hepatic venous outflow obstruction), with an underlying myeloproliferative neoplasm (JAK2-positive) as the prothrombotic driver (6). Cites the Doppler outflow obstruction plus the high-SAAG/high-protein ascites; explicitly excludes simple cirrhosis, viral/toxic hepatitis, and right-heart failure (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Budd-Chiari syndrome (hepatic venous outflow obstruction), with the JAK2 V617F-positive myeloproliferative neoplasm as the prothrombotic driver: supported by the Doppler outflow obstruction with caudate hypertrophy and the high-SAAG/high-protein ascites. Ruled out simple cirrhosis, viral/toxic hepatitis, and right-heart failure/constrictive pericarditis.
mustNotMiss: Cirrhosis with decompensation / portal vein thrombosis
acceptableAlternatives: Malignancy with hepatic vein invasion (HCC) or peritoneal carcinomatosis

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH approach to hepatic venous outflow obstruction: start anticoagulation promptly (heparin then long-term) once bleeding is excluded (CANT-MISS,7); identify and treat the underlying prothrombotic disorder / MPN with hematology (4); manage ascites with sodium restriction and diuretics +/- therapeutic paracentesis (4); stepwise escalation — angioplasty/stenting or TIPS for refractory disease, transplant for fulminant failure (4); hepatology +/- interventional radiology consult (3); stop the oral contraceptive and avoid further prothrombotic exposures (3). PENALTY: anticoagulating in the face of active variceal/GI bleeding without addressing it (-3); leaving the patient on the estrogen-containing contraceptive (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Anticoagulation is the cornerstone: start heparin (transition to long-term anticoagulation) once active bleeding/varices are excluded. Identify and treat the underlying thrombophilia/myeloproliferative neoplasm with hematology. Manage ascites with sodium restriction, diuretics, and therapeutic paracentesis as needed. Escalate in a stepwise fashion: angioplasty/stenting of a focal stenosis or TIPS for refractory disease, and liver transplant for fulminant hepatic failure. Stop the estrogen-containing oral contraceptive. Hepatology and interventional radiology consults; admit.

coreActions.item: Identify and treat the underlying prothrombotic disorder / MPN
Manage ascites (sodium restriction, diuretics, therapeutic paracentesis)
Stepwise escalation (angioplasty/stent or TIPS; transplant for fulminant failure)

penalties.item: Anticoagulating with active variceal/GI bleed without addressing it

optionalActions: Identify and treat the underlying prothrombotic disorder / MPN
Manage ascites (sodium restriction, diuretics, therapeutic paracentesis)
Stepwise escalation (angioplasty/stent or TIPS; transplant for fulminant failure)
Surveillance for hepatocellular carcinoma and varices over time
unsafeActions: Anticoagulating with active variceal/GI bleed without addressing it

historyTriggers.response: The belly swelling and the ache on the right side came on gradually over about three weeks, and my legs started swelling in the last week.
It's a dull, constant ache up under my right ribs, and my belly feels tight and full. I get full quickly when I eat.
I've felt a bit nauseous and my appetite is poor. No vomiting. I've noticed my eyes look a little yellow.
Yes, I started a combined birth control pill about eight months ago. I take it every day.
I've had two early miscarriages, both in the first few months. We've never had a successful pregnancy.
My sister has had blood clots in her legs, and my brother was told he has some kind of 'thick blood' disorder.
No, I haven't had any black or bloody stools, and I've never vomited blood.
I barely drink — maybe a couple of glasses of wine in a whole month.
No, my thinking is clear. I haven't been confused or unusually sleepy.
No known liver problems, no hepatitis that I'm aware of, no IV drugs, no tattoos, and no blood transfusions.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Abdomen: tender, smooth hepatomegaly in the RUQ.
Abdomen: tense ascites with shifting dullness and a positive fluid wave if examined.
Neuro: alert and oriented; NO asterixis (no encephalopathy).

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 32F on combined oral contraceptives presents with 3 weeks of progressive abdominal distension, right upper quadrant pain, and leg swelling. The diagnosis is Budd-Chiari syndrome (hepatic venous outflow obstruction), reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyHistoryClues: Two first-trimester pregnancy losses (possible antiphospholipid syndrome)
Family history of venous thrombosis and a 'thick blood' disorder (inherited/myeloproliferative thrombophilia)
Subacute triad of abdominal pain, distension/ascites, and tender hepatomegaly
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: tender, smooth hepatomegaly in the RUQ.
Abdomen: tense ascites with shifting dullness and a positive fluid wave if examined.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Budd-Chiari syndrome while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Budd-Chiari syndrome and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on simple cirrhosis and never imaging the hepatic veins
Anticoagulating with active variceal/GI bleed without addressing it
Leaving the patient on the estrogen-containing oral contraceptive
managementPearls: Identify and treat the underlying prothrombotic disorder / MPN
Stepwise escalation (angioplasty/stent or TIPS; transplant for fulminant failure)
criticalWorkupItems: Diagnostic paracentesis with SAAG and ascitic protein
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia with low limb-lead QRS voltage; no acute ischemic changes.
Small right pleural effusion with elevated hemidiaphragms (hepatic hydrothorax).
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with low limb-lead QRS voltage; no acute ischemic changes.
Small right pleural effusion with elevated hemidiaphragms (hepatic hydrothorax).
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with low limb-lead QRS voltage; no acute ischemic changes.
Small right pleural effusion with elevated hemidiaphragms (hepatic hydrothorax).
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
